---
title: "Path to Databases"
subtitle: "The mathematics that lets a query return the right rows in the right order, fast"
---

## What you can do at the end of this path

- Read and write relational algebra; understand a query plan as an algebraic
  expression.
- Reason about cost models: how a query optimiser decides between a hash join
  and a merge join.
- Understand the set-theoretic foundations of SQL — including the surprising
  parts (NULLs, three-valued logic).

## The path

Databases live on set theory, logic, and combinatorics. Statistics enter for
cost estimation and learned indexes.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Tier 1<br/>Fractions, data & average] --> B[Tier 2<br/>Functions as machines]
    B --> C[Tier 3<br/>Combinatorics, basic probability]
    C --> D[Tier 5<br/>Discrete math: sets, logic, relations<br/>Probability & statistics]
    D --> E[Tier 6<br/>Graph theory, optimisation]
    E --> F[Tier 7 — Databases track:<br/>Relational algebra<br/>Query optimisation<br/>Set-theoretic foundations]

    classDef built fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    class A built
```

## Where to start right now

The database path begins with **Fractions as Parts** (a row is a fraction of a
table) and **Data & Average** (the simplest aggregate query) in Tier 1.

## What's built / what's planned

- ✅ Tier 1: *What is a Number?* (exemplar lesson)
- 📋 Everything beyond — planned, not yet authored.
